coredata.dc:title: Self-Standing Hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO with Unprecedented Capacity and Stability for Lithium and Sodium Storage
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jianbin
Jiang, Zhuoheng
Niu, Shuwen
Zhu, Shanshan
Zhou, Jie
Zhu, Yongchun
Liang, Jianwen
Han, Dongdong
Xu, Kangli
Zhu, Linqin
Liu, Xiaojing
Wang, Gongming
Qian, Yitai
coredata.dc:description: Red phosphorus (P) suffers from large volume variation and poor conductivity as anodes for lithium and sodium ion batteries. Moreover, the currently used evaporation-condensation preparation method raises serious safety concerns, and ball-milled P/C also gives poor performance because of interparticle adhesion. Here, we present a mild solution synthesis of unique hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO, which not only achieves maximum conductivity in red P agglomerates but also provides volume variation space to relieve the pulverization issues. Even at a high P content of 70 wt %, P/CNTs@rGO still exhibits a specific capacity of 2,189.1 mA hr g−1 for lithium-ion batteries and 2,112.9 mA hr g−1 for sodium-ion batteries. These values are very close to the theoretical specific capacity. More importantly, it achieves an unprecedented gravimetric capacity of 1,532.4 mA hr g−1 on the basis of the mass of the whole electrode, as well as excellent cycling performance for 500 cycles, representing the best Li-storage performance among red-P-based electrodes to date.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SDG7: Affordable and clean energy
red phosphorus
energy storage
self-standing hierarchical electrode
graphene
lithium-ion batteries
sodium-ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and Characterization of the Self-Standing P/CNTs@rGO
Electrochemical Performance of the Self-Standing P/CNTs@rGO as Anodes for LIBs and SIBs

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This article is made available under the Elsevier license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Synthesis and Characterization of P/CNTs@rGO
(A) Schematic illustration of the preparation process of P/CNTs@rGO composites.
(B) Photograph of P/CNTs in water solution, GO solution, P/CNT/GO solution, and the assembled P/CNTs@rGO hydrogel in 20 mL glass vials.
(C) Photograph of the assembled P/CNTs@rGO hydrogels with different P content.
(D) Photograph of the kilogram-scale preparation of P/CNT powder in the laboratory.
(E) Demonstration of a self-standing P/CNTs@rGO hydrogel assembled in a 3-L container.

(F–M) SEM (top row) and TEM (bottom row) images of ball-milled red P nanoparticles (F and G), P/CNTs (H and I), P@rGO (J and K), and P/CNTs@rGO (L and M). The inset in (L) is a flexible and self-standing P/CNTs@rGO paper electrode.
Structure and Chemical State Characterization
(A and B) The XRD patterns (A) and Raman shifts (B) of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO.
(C) High-resolution spectra of XPS P 2p of P/CNTs and P/CNTs@rGO.
(D) FTIR spectrum of P/CNTs@rGO.
Electrochemical Characterization of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO as Anodes for LIBs
(A) The initial galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO at 0.2 C.
(B) Discharge capacities of P/CNTs@rGO with different P content (40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 wt %) at 0.2 C.
(C) The cycling performance of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO at 0.5 C for 100 cycles.
(D) The rate performance of P/CNTs@rGO at rates varying from 0.2 to 10.0 C.
(E) The long-term cycling performance of P/CNTs@rGO at 1.0 C over 500 cycles.
(F) Comparison of whole electrode gravimetric capacities; see also Table S2.
(G) The potential response curves of P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO during GITT measurements. The inset is the magnified area illustrating the overpotential.
(H) In situ reaction resistances of P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO during charge-discharge processes.
Electrochemical Performance of P/CNTs@rGO as Anodes for SIBs
(A) The potential versus capacity profiles of P/CNTs@rGO for the first three cycles at 0.2 C.
(B) The rate performance of P/CNTs@rGO at rates varying from 0.2 to 6.0 C.
(C) The cycling performance of P/CNTs@rGO at 1.0 C for 500 cycles.
The Electrochemical Characterization of the Full Cell with P/CNTs@rGO as the Anode and LiCoO2 as the Cathode
(A) Schematic structure of the full battery.
(B) Digital photographs of a red LED lit by the full cell under flat and bent states.
(C) The cycling performance of the full cell at 0.2 C over 100 cycles.
(D) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the full cell at the 1st, 5th, 50th, and 100th cycles. The specific capacity was calculated on the basis of P.
Schematic Illustration of Different Red P Hybrids before and after Lithiation or Sodiation
Ball-milled red P agglomerates (A), red P/CNTs (B), and hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO (C) before and after lithiation or sodiation.

head.ce:title: Self-Standing Hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO with Unprecedented Capacity and Stability for Lithium and Sodium Storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Science at Microscale and Department of Chemistry, University of Science & Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Science at Microscale and Department of Chemistry
University of Science & Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red phosphorus (P) suffers from large volume variation and poor conductivity as anodes for lithium and sodium ion batteries. Moreover, the currently used evaporation-condensation preparation method raises serious safety concerns, and ball-milled P/C also gives poor performance because of interparticle adhesion. Here, we present a mild solution synthesis of unique hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO, which not only achieves maximum conductivity in red P agglomerates but also provides volume variation space to relieve the pulverization issues. Even at a high P content of 70 wt %, P/CNTs@rGO still exhibits a specific capacity of 2,189.1 mA hr g−1 for lithium-ion batteries and 2,112.9 mA hr g−1 for sodium-ion batteries. These values are very close to the theoretical specific capacity. More importantly, it achieves an unprecedented gravimetric capacity of 1,532.4 mA hr g−1 on the basis of the mass of the whole electrode, as well as excellent cycling performance for 500 cycles, representing the best Li-storage performance among red-P-based electrodes to date.

With ever-increasing demands for large-capacity batteries, red phosphorus (P), which has a high theoretical specific capacity (∼2,600 mA hr g−1) and low material cost, holds great interest for the next generation of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and sodium-ion batteries (SIBs). However, red P suffers from poor cycling performance, and the currently used preparation methods raise either serious safety concerns or significant particle aggregation. Here, we report a unique P/CNTs@rGO hierarchical structure to fully explore ball-milled commercial red P as anodes for LIBs and SIBs. The self-standing hierarchical structure not only improves the conductivity of the agglomerates to resolve the interparticle adhesion issues of ball-milled P but also provides extra space to relieve the material pulverization. More importantly, the P/CNTs@rGO electrode, without using polymer binders and metallic current collectors, offers an ideal platform for pushing the gravimetric capacity of the whole electrode to the upper experimental limit.

Red phosphorus (P), which has a high theoretical specific capacity (∼2,600 mA hr g−1), low cost, and commercial availability, has been severely limited by large volume variation and poor electrical conductivity for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and sodium-ion batteries (SIBs). Here, we fabricate a self-standing P/CNTs@rGO hierarchical structure to effectively resolve the critical issues associated with red P. The self-standing P/CNTs@rGO electrode achieves an unprecedented capacity and thus represents an advanced step toward high-performance P-based anodes for LIBs and SIBs.

ce:section.ce:para: Red phosphorus (P) has been regarded as one of the most promising anodes for the next generation of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) because of its high theoretical specific capacity (∼2,600 mA hr g−1), low cost, and commercial availability. 1–5 However, the large volume variation (∼300%) and the low electrical conductivity (∼10−10 S cm−1) of red P result in severe electrode pulverization and an unstable solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) membrane during repeated charge-discharge processes, thus leading to fast capacity fading and substantially hindering its steps toward practical battery applications. 6–8 To this end, P/C composites have been extensively developed to improve the electrochemical performance of red P. 9–14 For instance, Li et al. 14 used an evaporation-condensation method to prepare P@CMK-3 with a low P content of 31 wt % and reported a capacity of ∼2,240 mA hr g−1 at 0.25 C for LIBs and 2,188 mA hr g−1 at 0.2 C for SIBs. However, with the increase in the P loading content, the specific capacities and cycling performance of red P/C composites decreased rapidly. 11 Moreover, thermal evaporation of red P inevitably generates the side product of white P, which eventually requires organic solvents or alkaline solutions to remove the white P from P/C composites. 14 White P, which has a low ignition temperature (30°C) and high toxicity, could raise serious safety concerns for the scalable production of red P via the vaporization method.

On the other hand, ball milling is one of the most commonly used techniques for large-scale material preparation in industry. 15,16 Importantly, ball milling can break up commercial red P into nanosized particles without involving thermal evaporation and generation of white P. Unfortunately, the overall performance of ball-milled red P is far from satisfactory, compared with red P prepared by the well-used vaporization method. 17–21 For example, Park and Sohn 20 found the cycling performance of ball-milled red P for LIBs was extremely poor and only 10% of the initial capacity was maintained over 30 cycles. Even adding structural stability agents, nearly 50% loss over 100 cycles was still observed with ball-milled P/C composites. 21 The unsatisfactory performance is attributed to the intrinsic polymeric structural feature of red P, which easily forms large agglomerates by interparticle adhesion during ball milling, 22,23 as shown in Scheme 1 A. The large agglomerates, which have a limited contact area between P and conducting carbon additives, still suffer from low conductivity and structural pulverization, leading to the low capacity and poor cycling performance. Although increasing the amount of conducting carbon additives in the P/C composites can improve the electrochemical performance of red P to some extent, the low ratio of active material affects its potential for practical applications. Safely and efficiently utilizing ball-milled commercial red P and pushing the performance to a higher level for practical application is always a great challenge.

Here, we rationally designed and fabricated a unique hierarchical structure to fully explore ball-milled commercial red P as anode for LIBs and SIBs and push the gravimetric capacity of the whole electrode to the upper experimental limit. To resolve the low conductivity in the inner part of the large agglomerates induced by interparticle adhesion, we used carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with excellent flexibility and conductivity to interweave with the red P agglomerates (Scheme 1B). To avoid the detachment of red P nanoparticles from CNTs due to volume variation during cycling and further maximize the contact area between agglomerates and conducting carbon, we used a three-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide (rGO) network with space for volume expansion to encapsulate the P/CNTs agglomerates (P/CNTs@rGO), as shown in Scheme 1C. More importantly, the atomically thin graphene nanosheets could potentially offer the theoretical minimum amount of carbon additives but the maximum contact area between carbon and red P, achieving the highest loading content of red P and maximized conductivity in P/C composites. Furthermore, the self-standing network can also avoid using polymer binders and metallic current collectors, providing an ideal platform for pushing the gravimetric capacity of the whole electrode to the upper experimental limit. 24–27 In this work, we have demonstrated the scalable production of self-standing P/CNTs@rGO with a tunable content of red P via a mild self-assembly approach without involving any vaporization process. P/CNTs@rGO exhibited a specific capacity of 2,189.1 mA hr g−1 for LIBs and 2,112.9 mA hr g−1 for SIBs even at the high P content of 70 wt %, very close to the theoretical specific capacity of red P. More importantly, it also achieved an unprecedented gravimetric capacity of 1,532.4 mA hr g−1 at 0.42 A g−1 on the basis of the mass of the whole electrode, as well as excellent cycling performance for 500 cycles, representing the upper experimental limit of P/C composites that can be achieved to date. Even assembled with LiCoO2, P/CNTs@rGO also showed a high capacity retention of 1,362.4 mA hr g−1, suggesting superior electrochemical properties in a full-cell operation. Moreover, the design principle of such hierarchical structures can also be effectively applied to other electrode materials with highly improved electrochemical performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and Characterization of the Self-Standing P/CNTs@rGO
ce:section.ce:para: The self-standing P/CNTs@rGO was prepared via a three-step process, as illustrated in Figure 1 A. Firstly, commercial red P powder was ball milled with multi-walled CNTs with a weight ratio of 9:1 for the preparation of P/CNTs. Then, the prepared P/CNTs were uniformly dispersed in graphene oxide solution (P/CNTs/GO). Finally, the self-standing P/CNTs@rGO hydrogel was obtained by a self-assembly process induced by ascorbic acid at 90°C. Figure 1B shows the corresponding digital pictures of P/CNTs dispersed in water solution, GO solution, P/CNTs/GO solution, and the self-assembled P/CNTs@rGO hydrogel in 20 mL glass vials. Impressively, the hydrogel has a well-interconnected network and good mechanical strength. To acquire the upper content limit of red P in the P/CNTs@rGO hydrogel, we tried the assembly process by using different P content (40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90 wt %). The images of the self-assembled products are displayed in Figure 1C. The P/CNTs/GO solution does not assemble into self-standing hydrogel only at 90 wt %. This suggests that the upper limit to form a well-interconnected network is 80 wt %, and rGO cannot form a continuous network when the P content is too high. Moreover, ball-milling preparation and the self-assembly process can be easily scaled up. Figure 1D shows a kilogram-scale preparation of P/CNTs, and Figure 1E displays the self-standing P/CNTs@rGO hydrogel assembled in a 3-L container, exhibiting the tremendous advantages toward large-scale production under such mild conditions.

To evaluate the structural benefits of P/CNTs@rGO, we used scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the existential states of each component in P/CNTs@rGO. Figure S1 displays the SEM image of commercial red P powder without ball-milling treatment. The particle size of commercial red P is in the range of tens of micrometers. After ball milling, the particle size of red P is significantly decreased to hundreds of nanometers, as shown in Figure 1F. The TEM image in Figure 1G further reveals that ball-milled red P particles are composed of numerous smaller particles tens of nanometers in size or even smaller, clearly indicating the existence of interparticle adhesion to form large agglomerates during ball milling, because of the polymeric structural feature of red P. 23 After adding CNTs as additives, the SEM and TEM images of P/CNTs hybrids (Figures 1H and 1I) clearly show that the CNTs can thrust into the large agglomerates of red P and weave the smaller nanoparticles together leading to large agglomerates with improved conductivity. Even so, the contact area between CNTs and nanoparticles is still too limited to achieve satisfactory conductivity, and the woven P nanoparticles can detach from the CNTs during volume variation. To this end, an atomically thin graphene nanosheet with excellent flexibility and conductivity is a good candidate to encapsulate the agglomerates and further increase the contact area. The SEM and TEM images of P@rGO (Figures 1J and 1K) reveal that red P agglomerates can be well embedded in the graphene network, suggesting intimate contact around the agglomerates. Using the synergistic benefits of graphene encapsulation and CNT entanglement, P/CNTs@rGO (Figures 1L and 1M) can form a three-dimensional hierarchical conducting network to endow the entire electrode with maximized conductivity; CNTs thrust into the red P agglomerates to improve the inner conductivity of the agglomerates and graphene sheets encapsulate the red P agglomerates to make the outer shell conducting. In addition, the flexible and porous features of the graphene network provide extra space for volume expansion and avoid electrode pulverization. 28,29 Moreover, P/CNTs@rGO can be further pressed into a flexible and self-standing paper electrode (Figure 1L, inset) without the addition of any other polymer binders or loading on metal current collectors, which can substantially decrease the total mass of the whole electrode for battery applications.

The structures and the chemical states of P/CNTs@rGO were further characterized. Figure 2 A shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ball-milled P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO. All the XRD patterns present a distinct peak located at 16° and a broad hump in the range of 28°–35°, which match well with those of commercial amorphous red P (Figure S2) and red P reported in previous work. 30 High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Figure S3A) and selected area electron diffraction (Figure S3B) images also prove the amorphous structure of ball-milled red P. The P/CNTs exhibit one more peak at 26°, because of the presence of CNTs. 31 P@rGO and P/CNTs@rGO display a broad peak located at 22°–28°, indicating the presence of rGO. 32,33 We used Raman spectroscopy to characterize P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO composites (Figure 2B). The puckered three-membered peaks in the range of 300–500 cm−1 appearing in all the spectra can be assigned to characteristics of red P, 34 and the existence of a D band at 1,350 cm−1 and a G band at 1,585 cm−1 suggests the presence of carbon in P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO. 35–37 The relative lower intensities of Raman peaks for red P in P@rGO and P/CNTs@rGO are attributed to the surface coverage with rGO, suggesting red P is well embedded in the rGO network. We conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to probe the chemical states of the samples. Figure 2C displays the high-resolution XPS P2p spectra of P/CNTs and P/CNTs@rGO. The distinct peaks centered at around 130.1 and 134.5 eV can be indexed to the P–P bond and P–O bond, respectively. 38 The relative intensity of P–O bonds in P/CNTs@rGO is much higher than that of P/CNTs as a result of the strong chemical interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups on rGO and red P, which is believed to be helpful for maintaining the intimate contact between P and rGO and the interconnected architecture. 39 Furthermore, the surface chemical states were proved by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, as shown in Figure 2D. The peaks at 1,160 and 1,050 cm−1 can be assigned to P=O and P–O bonds as a result of the exposure of P nanoparticles in the air. 40 Notably, the peak centered at 1,008 cm−1 suggests the formation of a P–O–C bond, 41 also indicating the strong interaction between rGO, CNTs, and red P. To quantitatively evaluate the P content in the P/CNTs@rGO electrode, we conducted thermogravimetry (TG). As illustrated in Figure S4, the measured P content of 69.3 wt % is consistent with the feeding ratio of P (70 wt %), suggesting that red P can be efficiently trapped in the graphene network during the self-assembly process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical Performance of the Self-Standing P/CNTs@rGO as Anodes for LIBs and SIBs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performance of the self-standing P/CNTs@rGO as anodes for LIBs was evaluated in a half-cell configuration with metallic lithium foil as the counter electrode within the voltage window of 0.01–2.0 V (see details in the Experimental Procedures). Figure 3 A shows the initial galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO electrodes with P content of 70 wt % at 0.2 C (1 C = 2.6 A g−1). The self-standing P@rGO and P/CNTs@rGO were used directly as electrodes for testing without the inclusion of any polymer binders or metal current collectors, and the red P and P/CNTs electrodes with the same red P content were fabricated with polyvinylidene fluoride as binder, super P as conducting agent, and copper foil as current collector. The specific capacity was calculated on the basis of the weight of red P unless otherwise noted. During the discharge process, all electrodes displayed two main potential plateaus at around 0.6–0.8 V and below 0.4 V, corresponding to the lithiation processes from P to Li x P and Li3P. 42 Two main voltage plateaus at around 1.0 V and 1.2 V in the charge process were also observed, assigned to reverse delithiation from Li3P to Li x P and P. 43 These observations are consistent with the results from cyclic voltammogram (CV) studies, which exhibited peaks at around 0.71, 0.17, and 0.01 V during the cathodic sweep and 1.0, 1.2, and 1.27 V during the anodic sweep, as shown in Figure S5. All four electrodes displayed similar initial discharge features and capacities but totally different charge profiles. The initial charge capacity of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO at 0.2 C was 145.4, 1,449.4, 1,877.8, and 2,186.8 mA hr g−1, respectively, corresponding to an initial Coulombic efficiency of 5.3%, 54.0%, 71.3%, and 83.8%, respectively. Compared with the other three electrodes, the P/CNTs@rGO electrode showed much higher initial capacity and Coulombic efficiency. The excellent initial Coulombic efficiency could be attributed to the relatively low surface area (∼126 m2/g, as shown in Figure S6). In addition, the conductivity and structural stability of electrodes can also affect the initial Coulombic efficiency. Compared with the P/CNTs and P@rGO, P/CNTs@rGO with fully maximized electrical conductivity and superior structural stability during cycling could help keep the integrity of the formed SEI membrane and the interconnected hierarchical structures, thus achieving better initial Coulombic efficiency. We further studied the effects of the P content on the electrochemical performance of P/CNTs@rGO. Figure 3B shows the specific capacity of P/CNTs@rGO with red P contents of 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 wt % at 0.2 C. Interestingly, with increasing red P content from 40 to 70 wt %, P/CNTS@rGO electrodes exhibited similar specific capacities consistently higher than 2,150 mA hr g−1. Even at the maximum content of 80 wt %, P/CNTs@rGO still delivered a specific capacity of 2,056.3 mA hr g−1, which represented the highest capacity achieved by red P electrodes at such a high loading ratio (80 wt %), indicating the unique benefits of hierarchical networks to maintain superior electrochemical performance at high loading content. 2 The weight ratio of CNTs and rGO was also optimized. We prepared P/CNTs@rGO electrodes with fixed red P content of 70 wt % and various weight ratios of CNTs:rGO and studied their electrochemical performance, as summarized in Table S1 and Figure S7. P/CNTs@rGO with rGO:CNTs = 2.75:1 showed the best electrochemical performance, suggesting optimized electrical conductivity and mechanical strength of the self-standing structure. With fewer CNTs in P/CNTs@rGO, CNTs cannot cross link the agglomerated P nanoparticles as well. With a further increased ratio of CNTs, the mechanical strength of the self-standing structure cannot be well maintained to relieve the volume variation of P during charge-discharge processes. Cycling stability is another major issue affecting red-P-based electrodes. The cycling performance of P, P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO electrodes with P contents of 70 wt % was firstly evaluated at 0.5 C for 100 cycles, as shown in Figure 3C; 0.2 C was applied in the first three cycles to sufficiently activate P before the cycling test. Compared with the other three control counterparts, P/CNTs@rGO displayed the best cycling performance. After 100 cycles, the remaining specific capacity for the P, P/CNTs, and P@rGO electrodes at 0.5 C was only 31.4, 165.5, and 920.6 mA hr g−1, respectively. As a comparison, the P/CNTs@rGO electrode maintained a specific capacity of 1,725.3 mA hr g−1 after 100 cycles, revealing a high-capacity retention of 98.0% from the fourth cycle. These results indicate that the three-dimensional hierarchical structure of P/CNTs@rGO can effectively relieve the cycling issues induced by low conductivity and large volume variation of red P during charge-discharge processes.

We also studied the rate capabilities of the P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO electrodes with various charge-discharge rates. Figure S8 shows the rate performance of P/CNTs and P@rGO electrodes with current densities varying from 0.2 to 10.0 C; both showed fast capacity fading with the increase in charge-discharge rates, such that they maintained only 96 and 212.9 mA hr g−1 at 10.0 C, respectively. Moreover, the capacity could not be restored, even when the rate returned to 0.5 C. As a comparison, the discharge capacities of P/CNTs@rGO electrode were 2,189.1, 1769.1, 1,492.7, 1,226.8, 1,082.7, 913.3, 704.3, and 452 mA hr g−1 at rates of 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 8.0, and 10.0 C, respectively (Figure 3D). When the rate returned to 0.5 C, a discharge capacity of 1,608.4 mA hr g−1 was restored. The superior rate capability of P/CNTs@rGO is attributed to the three-dimensional hierarchical conducting network, which can not only facilitate fast ion diffusion but also endow red P with excellent conductivity and offer extra space for volume variation. P/CNTs@rGO was also tested for even longer cycles at 1.0 C (Figure 3E). Even after 500 cycles, it still delivered a high capacity of 1,108.4 mA hr g−1 with a low-capacity fading rate of 0.08% per cycle from the fourth cycle. More importantly, considering the self-standing P/CNTs@rGO can be used directly as a battery electrode without the addition of a polymer binder or a metal current collector, it offers an ideal platform for pushing the gravimetric capacity of the whole electrode to the upper experimental limit with high P content. The self-standing P/CNTs@rGO electrode can deliver a high gravimetric capacity of 1,532.4 mA hr g−1 at 0.4 A g−1, 1,207.7 mA hr g−1 at 0.9 A g−1, and 775.9 mA hr g−1 at 1.8 A g−1, which is several times higher than that of red-P-based electrodes prepared by conventional vaporization methods and represents the best performance to date, as shown in Figure 3F. The detailed comparison and calculations are summarized in Table S2. In addition, the calculated areal capacities of the P/CNTs@rGO electrode are 3.3 mA hr cm−2 (volumetric capacity: 1,094.5 mA hr cm−3) at an areal current density of 0.78 mA cm−2 and 2.65 mA hr cm−2 (volumetric capacity: 884.6 mA hr cm−3) at 2.0 mA cm−2 for LIBs.

To further reveal the structural advantages to improve the electrochemical properties of P/CNTs@rGO electrodes for LIBs, we used electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT). Figure S9 shows the EIS spectra and Warburg coefficient plots of P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO. The semicircle in the high-frequency region of the Nyquist plot is related to the interfacial charge-transfer resistance, and the diagonal line in the low-frequency region originates from the ionic diffusion impedance. In comparison with P@rGO and P/CNTs@rGO, P/CNTs display a large diameter of the semicircle (Figure S9A), suggesting that rGO plays a crucial role in improving the electrical conductivity of the electrodes and decreasing the interfacial charge-transfer resistance. In addition, the P/CNTs@rGO with the lowest interfacial charge-transfer resistance is attributed to the improved conductivity in the whole red P agglomerates with the assistance of the CNTs. The Warburg coefficient σ, which is the slope of the plot between Z′re with ω −1/2 (ω = 2πf), can be used to evaluate the ionic diffusion properties in the electrode. Figure S9B shows the Warburg coefficient σ plots of P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO. P/CNTs@rGO also exhibits the smallest σ value, suggesting superior ionic diffusion properties. We further used the GITT with both transient and steady-state measurements to monitor the reaction resistance evolution of P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO in situ during the charge-discharge process. During GITT measurements, we adopted a constant current density of 0.2 A g−1 for 1 hr to obtain the closed-circuit voltage and applied a pulse of duration of 5 hr to collect the quasi-open-circuit voltage. This measurement can be considered a quasi-static process, and the reaction resistance can thus be approximated. Figure 3G shows the voltage response of P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO electrodes during the first cycle. The reaction resistances were evaluated by the overpotentials, the potential drop between the end of the relaxation step and the start of the current step, which is highlighted in the inset of Figure 3G. The corresponding reaction resistances during discharge and charge processes are illustrated in Figure 3H. Typically, for P/CNTs, P@rGO, and P/CNTs@rGO, the reaction resistances decreased during the discharge process and increased during the charge process, which can be ascribed to the relatively better conductivity of the discharged product Li x P than that of the charged product P. 44,45 As expected, P/CNTs@rGO displayed the lowest reaction resistances during both the discharge and charge processes, indicating that P/CNTs@rGO has maximum electrical conductivity in the whole electrode. In addition, the structural stability also had critical effects on the electrochemical cycling performance. Figure S10A shows the SEM image of P/CNTs@rGO after cycling at 0.5 C over 100 cycles. The well-preserved porous 3D architecture and the red P particles tightly embedded in the graphene network after cycling indicate that the structure of P/CNTs@rGO is very robust during the cycling processes. We further used the FTIR spectrum of the P/CNTs@rGO electrode to probe the chemical state changes after cycling, as shown in Figure S10B. Although the P–O and P=O bonds could be still observed, the P–O–C bond almost disappeared in the spectrum, suggesting that the P–O–C bond cannot be well maintained during the cycling processes, and the chemical interaction between rGO and P is not a key factor for improving P-based electrodes for LIBs. On the basis of this comprehensive investigation, the excellent Li-storage performance of P/CNTs@rGO can be ascribed to the unique and robust three-dimensional hierarchical architecture, which not only improves the conductivity of the agglomerate of red P to solve the interparticle adhesion issues with ball-milled red P but also provides volume variation space to relieve the pulverization issues.

Furthermore, P/CNTs@rGO electrodes were also applied as anodes for SIBs. Figure S11 shows the CV curves of P/CNTs@rGO with a red P loading ratio of 70 wt % for the first two cycles with a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. In the cathodic process, three peaks located at around 0.3, 0.17, and 0.03 V can be ascribed to stepwise sodiation from P to Na x P and finally to Na3P. 46 In the backward process, the anodic peaks at around 0.55, 0.67, and 1.35 V correspond to desodiation from Na3P to P, 13 suggesting that the reaction is totally reversible. The voltage plateaus in the charge-discharge profiles of P/CNTs@rGO (Figure 4 A) during the first three cycles match well with the redox potentials observed in the CV curves. To evaluate the rate capability of P/CNTs@rGO, we studied the electrochemical performance with the rate varying from 0.2 to 6.0 C, as shown in Figure 4B. P/CNTs@rGO exhibited average specific capacities of 2,112.9, 1732, 1,464.3, 1,269.5, 1,059.6, 679, 449.1, and 286.9 mA hr g−1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 6.0 C, respectively. When the rate returned to 0.5 C, a high discharge capacity of 1,671.9 mA hr g−1 was restored. Moreover, P/CNTs@rGO electrodes displayed excellent cycling stability for 500 cycles, as shown in Figure 4C. We also summarized and compared our results with the electrochemical performance of red-P-based anodes reported previously in the literature, as shown in Table S3 and Figure S12. The hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO exhibited the best whole-electrode gravimetric capacities of 1,479 mA hr g−1 at 0.42 A g−1, 1,212.4 mA hr g−1 at 0.9 A g−1, and 728.6 mA hr g−1 at 1.8 A g−1. In addition, P/CNTs@rGO also displayed a high volumetric capacity of 1,056.5 mA hr cm−3 at 0.78 mA cm−2. All these results suggest that P/CNTs@rGO also have superior Na-storage capability, even as anodes for SIBs.

We have demonstrated the scalable synthesis of self-standing P/CNTs@rGO and its structural benefits as anodes evaluated in a half-cell system. We also used the self-standing and flexible P/CNTs@rGO to assemble a flexible full battery with LiCoO2 (2 × 2 cm2) as the cathode and P/CNTs@rGO (2 × 2 cm2) as the anode. Figure 5 A illustrates the schematic structure of the full battery, which is packed and sealed in a plastic bag. Figure 5B shows the flexibility of the packaged full battery. The charged full battery can light a light emitting diode (LED) even under bending conditions, displaying its potential to be applied in flexible and portable energy storage devices. Figure 5C shows the galvanostatic charge-discharge behavior of full cells at 0.2 C. It reveals an initial charge and discharge capacity of 2,112.8 and 1,527.6 mA hr g−1, respectively, corresponding to a Coulombic efficiency of ∼72% according to the calculation of P. The loss of capacity can be attributed to the formation of an SEI membrane, which is normal for all commercial batteries. 47 Meaningfully, the self-standing P/CNTs@rGO exhibits a high discharge capacity retention of 1,362.4 mA hr g−1 in a full cell even after 100 cycles. The charge-discharge voltage profiles at the 1st, 5th, 50th, and 100th cycles are shown in Figure 5D. The main discharge potential plateau of the full cell is around 2.9 V, which is consistent with the potential difference between the discharge plateau of LiCoO2 and red P (3.6 V for LiCoO2 and 0.7 V for P). More importantly, the calculated energy density of the full cell is 321.3 W hr kg−1 (983.2 W hr L−1), which is much higher than that of full batteries reported recently (1.3 times higher than a Si-based full cell, 48 1.9 times higher than a graphite-based full cell, and 10 times higher than a P-based full cell, 8 calculation shown in Figure S13), suggesting the self-standing P/CNTs@rGO holds great promise for full battery application.

To further demonstrate the structural advantages, we also extended this unique structure in a silicon anode and a sulfur cathode for LIBs. Figure S14A shows a typical SEM image of Si/CNT composites after ball milling, revealing that CNTs are also homogeneously entangled with Si nanoparticles. As anode for LIBs (Figures S14B and S14C), a Si/CNTs@rGO electrode exhibits excellent cycling performance with a high capacity retention of 2,138.6 mA hr g−1 at 0.5 C (1 C = 3.6 A g−1) over 100 cycles, which is also higher than that of Si@rGO. A S/CNTs composite was also prepared according to the same process (Figure S14D). After 100 cycles, the S/CNTs@rGO electrode (Figures S14E and S14F) shows superior cycling stability and maintains a capacity of 949.9 mA hr g−1 at 0.5 C (1 C = 1.67 A g−1), indicating the unique structural design is a general approach to improve the electrochemical performance of electrode materials for battery applications.

In summary, we have developed a scalable method for preparing a self-standing hierarchical P/CNTs@rGO, which not only improves the conductivity of agglomerated red P to solve the interparticle adhesion issues of ball-milled P but also provides volume variation space to relieve the pulverization issues. The self-standing P/CNTs@rGO, which has a high loading amount (70 wt %) of P, delivers a gravimetric capacity of 1,532.4 mA hr g−1 on the basis of the whole-electrode mass for LIBs, which represents the highest value among red-P-based anodes for LIBs to date. Moreover, P/CNTs@rGO also displays excellent cycling performance for 500 cycles at 1.0 C. Even assembled in a full battery with LiCoO2, P/CNTs@rGO can still maintain a highly reversible discharge capacity of 1,362.4 mA hr g−1 and deliver an unprecedented energy density of 321.3 W hr kg−1 at 0.2 C. P/CNTs@rGO also exhibits superior Na-storage performance with a high capacity retention of 2,112.9 mA hr g−1 at 0.2 C and excellent long-term cycling performance over 500 cycles. The design principle of P/CNTs@rGO could provide new opportunities to improve the electrochemical properties of red P for battery applications and beyond.
Detailed experimental procedures are provided in the Supplemental Information.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (BX201700221), the Natural Science Fund of China (11704365, GG2060190212, 21471142, 21671183, and 21671181), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0206703 and 2016YFB0901500), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WK2060190074, WK2060190081, and WK2060190053), start-up funding from the University of Science & Technology of China, the Recruitment Program of Global Experts, and the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1608085MB22). We also appreciate the Photoemission Endstations (BL10B) of the National Synchrotron Radiation Laboratory for the analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: G.M.W., Y.T.Q., and Y.C.Z. designed and directed the project. J.B.Z. and Z.H.J. conducted the project and contributed equally to this work. S.W.N., S.S.Z., J.Z., D.D.H., and L.Q.Z. contributed to the synthesis of graphene oxides and 3D graphene and the characterization of materials. J.W.L. and K.L.X. helped perform the GITT measurements. X.J.L. helped analyze the electrochemical properties of the samples. All authors contributed to the discussion of the manuscript. G.M.W. and J.B.Z. wrote the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental Information includes Supplemental Experimental Procedures, 14 figures, and 3 tables and can be found with this article online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chempr.2018.01.006.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Supplemental Experimental Procedures, Figures S1–S14, and Table S1–S3
Document S2. Article plus Supplemental Information
